nativeRange.habitat: Lagoons and turbid coastal reef environments

diet.notes: Goniopora hosts zooxanthellae — symbiotic algae living within its tissue that convert light into energy — which supply a portion of the coral's nutritional needs. Supplemental feeding of small coral foods (zooplankton, phytoplankton, or coral-specific liquid preparations) mixed with saltwater several times per week is generally advised and has been shown to improve long-term health and growth.

summary: The Flowerpot Coral, known scientifically as Goniopora, is a large-polyp stony coral prized for its dramatic, long-extended polyps that billow like flowers when healthy. It needs stable water chemistry, moderate light and flow, and supplemental feedings, placing it firmly in intermediate reef-keeping territory.
careNotes: The Flowerpot Coral belongs to the family Poritidae and, despite its soft, flowing appearance, builds a hard calcium carbonate skeleton — the defining trait of stony corals. Its long polyps extend dramatically during the day, giving the colony a flower-like look, and will retract noticeably if the animal is stressed by poor flow, inappropriate lighting, or shifts in water chemistry. The coral is best placed on a sand substrate at the lower level of the aquarium, where its skeleton can develop into rounded formations. Small aquacultured fragments may also be attached to rockwork. Maintain stable calcium (380–450 ppm), alkalinity (8–12 dKH), and magnesium (1200–1400 ppm). Nitrate in the range of 1–20 ppm and phosphate between 0.01–0.1 ppm are generally acceptable. Although Goniopora hosts zooxanthellae — symbiotic algae living inside its tissue that provide energy through photosynthesis — regular target feeding of small coral foods (zooplankton, phytoplankton, or coral-specific liquid preparations) several times per week markedly improves long-term health and growth. Historically regarded as one of the more challenging large-polyp stony corals to keep long-term, aquaculture-raised specimens are generally considered considerably hardier than wild-caught individuals. When fully extended, tentacles can reach and sting adjacent corals; allow at least 10–15 cm of clearance from neighboring colonies. The genus comprises approximately 29 recognized species, which are commonly traded collectively under the Goniopora name.

additional.name: Bulk Reef Supply
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters, care level, and lighting. BRS lists lighting as medium-to-high (150-250+ PAR) vs. WWC medium only; conservative medium (150-250 PAR) adopted. Both sources agree on medium flow. BRS care level 'moderate' maps to 'intermediate'.
